script_system_append: Cast the rider as a dignified heavy-set or plus-size American senior age 60+ and keep the same rider across connected scenes. Follow the framing logic seen in c1 and c2: the seated rider and wheelchair should feel full-sized, grounded, and naturally proportioned, never toy-sized or oversized. The rider should visibly fill the seat with natural support, but the torso must not tower unrealistically above the chair.
script_user_append: Within one concept, make the three scenes read as three clearly different phases of the same route. Scene 1 must show the home threshold or start point. Scene 2 must show the middle route with a clearly different environmental signature, such as enclosed greenery or the ramp itself. Scene 3 must show an arrival space with more open air, more sky, or level-ground destination cues. Keep the same screen direction from shot to shot so the edit feels like one believable outing instead of three repeated clips.
scene_pic_system_append: Use the same wheelchair, rider identity, spouse identity, wardrobe palette, and body proportions in every connected shot. Match the visual proportion cues from c1 and c2: medium or medium-wide portrait framing, most of the seated body and most of the wheelchair visible, minimal empty headroom, and a substantial wide seat that visibly supports a plus-size senior naturally. Do not let the wheelchair read tiny under the rider, and do not let the rider read shrunken inside an oversized chair.
scene_pic_user_append: Each concept must have clear scene differentiation. Scene 1 should visibly include the house threshold or porch entry. Scene 2 should visibly move farther from the house and show a middle-route signature that is not repeated from scene 1, such as close shrubs, flowers, a narrow garden path, or a finished ramp. Scene 3 should visibly feel more open and farther out, with less house detail, more sidewalk or lane cues, and more sky or open space. Keep the chair in normal open riding position only. The rider should read as a heavy-set or plus-size American senior with a broad torso, fuller thighs, and a calm confident expression. Use front-side or joystick-side angles only. Keep the rear/top-back structure compact and realistic. No folded-chair action, no hand-folding action, no extreme wheel close-up, and no awkward anatomy. During self-operated motion, the right hand must visibly rest on the joystick.
video_prompt_append: Preserve c1 and c2 proportion logic in motion: the rider and wheelchair must move as one believable full-size unit. Keep the rider heavy-set and well-supported by the chair, with the wide seat reading broad and stable, not cartoonishly huge or tiny. The route must feel continuous from shot to shot with matched motion direction and natural last-frame continuation. Within each concept, the three scenes must be clearly different: scene 1 near the house start, scene 2 in the middle route with a distinct visual signature, and scene 3 in a more open arrival space. Do not let scene 2 or scene 3 repeat the same doorway, same patch of path, or same background density from scene 1. Frame the subject like a polished Meta ad: medium or medium-wide 9:16 lifestyle composition, realistic background, simple successful action, clean negative space for post-added captions, and a strong emotional finish without turning the video into a product packshot. If spoken narration is requested in the scene details, keep that narration short, clear, and active through most of the clip. The generated footage itself must contain no readable letters, no built-in subtitles, no fake UI text, and no gibberish text artifacts.
